skill.name: hypogenic
skill.description: Automated hypothesis generation and testing for tabular research datasets. The skill explains HypoGeniC, HypoRefine, and Union workflows for generating, refining, and evaluating LLM-assisted scientific hypotheses.
skill.summary: Generate, refine, and test research hypotheses from tabular data and literature sources.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly documentation false positives: weak-crypto and C2 keyword alerts match research text, method names, or prompt-template wording rather than malicious code. The real risks are legitimate but user-visible external operations: package installation, repository cloning, helper shell scripts, network links, and API-key environment configuration. No prompt injection attempt, credential exfiltration, hidden code execution, or confirmed malicious behavior was found in the reviewed files.

medium_findings.title: External Package and Repository Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill documentation instructs users to install the hypogenic package, clone external GitHub repositories, and run command-line workflows. These are not automatically executed by the skill, but they create supply-chain and network risks if users run them without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced commands are explicit and user-facing. The risk is operational rather than hidden because the commands are shown in documentation and require user execution.

medium_findings.title: Helper Shell Scripts for Literature Processing
medium_findings.description: The literature workflow tells users to run GROBID setup and service scripts before processing PDFs. The scripts are outside the reviewed files, so their behavior is not verifiable from this skill package.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command references are clear, but the target scripts are not present in the scanned files. This supports a medium review warning, not a malicious finding.

medium_findings.title: API Key Environment Variable Configuration
medium_findings.description: The configuration template references an OpenAI API key environment variable for hosted model access. This is a normal integration pattern, but users should protect secrets and avoid committing environment values.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file names an environment variable for an API key but does not contain an actual secret or code that sends it elsewhere. The risk is secret-handling exposure during setup.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to GitHub, arXiv, ACL Anthology, PyPI, and issue pages used for installation, datasets, papers, and support. They are visible documentation links rather than covert network destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are plainly documented resources and are not embedded in executable code. Users still should review remote sources before cloning or installing.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and C2 Keyword Alerts Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography and C2 alerts occur in descriptive text, method names, YAML prompt fields, bibliography entries, or ordinary words. No cryptographic implementation, command-and-control behavior, or malicious beaconing evidence was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed contexts show research documentation and YAML templates, not crypto code or C2 logic. Confidence is high because the scanned package contains only documentation and a config template.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Check Found No Override Attempt
low_findings.description: No evidence found for instructions such as ignoring previous instructions, claiming pre-approval, changing risk levels, or impersonating system authority. A vendor-platform suggestion appears at the end of the documentation, but it is framed as an optional productivity suggestion for complex workflows.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search and manual review did not find direct override language. The vendor suggestion can steer assistant behavior, but it is not a clear prompt-injection attack.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Run Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes shell commands for installation, cloning datasets, starting services, and running CLI tools. These commands are visible and require user execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are explicit fenced examples. They are not hidden execution paths within the skill files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hosted Model Secret Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The configuration template uses an API-key environment variable name for hosted LLM access. No actual key value is present.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The API key environment variable is clearly documented. No code path in the reviewed files reads or transmits the secret.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Network Resources
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation links to external repositories, package indexes, papers, and support pages. These links are expected for a research tool but should be reviewed before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in documentation and point to normal research and package resources. They are not evidence of covert exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Research teams need structured ways to turn datasets and papers into testable hypotheses. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through HypoGeniC workflows for data-driven and literature-informed hypothesis testing.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use HypoGeniC, HypoRefine, and Union hypothesis workflows.
Documents dataset requirements for train, validation, and test splits.
Provides configuration guidance for prompt templates, labels, caching, and output settings.
Shows command-line and Python API examples for generation and inference workflows.
Describes literature preprocessing steps for PDF-based hypothesis extraction.
Outlines evaluation metrics and expected outputs for hypothesis testing.
limitations: Requires users to install and run the external hypogenic package themselves.
Needs valid research data, labels, and task-specific prompt templates before use.
May call external LLM APIs depending on the configured model provider.
Literature workflows require additional tools such as GROBID and PDF preprocessing.

use_cases.title: Explore New Dataset Patterns
use_cases.description: Generate candidate hypotheses from labeled tabular examples before building or evaluating predictive models.

use_cases.title: Combine Papers With Evidence
use_cases.description: Use literature-informed workflows to compare theory-driven hypotheses with patterns found in empirical data.

use_cases.title: Benchmark Hypothesis Quality
use_cases.description: Run generation and inference workflows to compare hypothesis banks across methods, metrics, and datasets.

prompt_templates.title: Start Data-Driven Generation
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for a prepared dataset.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the hypogenic skill to outline a HypoGeniC workflow for my labeled tabular dataset. Include required files, configuration steps, and expected outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Task Configuration
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate setup for a custom research task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a hypogenic configuration for this research task: [describe task]. Specify dataset fields, prompt templates, labels, and evaluation metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Generation Methods
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare HypoGeniC, HypoRefine, and Union for my project. Recommend one method and explain the data, literature, and tooling requirements.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Full Evaluation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an end-to-end experiment plan for generating, refining, deduplicating, and testing hypotheses across train, validation, and test splits.

output_examples.input: I have labeled social media posts and want hypotheses about stress indicators.
output: Recommended method: HypoGeniC for initial data-driven discovery.
Prepare train, validation, and test files with text features and labels.
Generate hypotheses, run inference, then inspect high-performing patterns for validity.

output_examples.input: I have ten papers and a deception detection dataset.
output: Recommended method: HypoRefine because the task has relevant prior literature.
Preprocess papers, configure literature prompts, and compare literature-only and data-driven hypothesis banks.
Use validation results to refine weak hypotheses before final testing.

output_examples.input: My generated hypotheses are generic and perform poorly.
output: Tighten prompt templates to request measurable, falsifiable hypotheses.
Review label extraction and ensure predictions match dataset labels.
Increase or rebalance examples used during refinement.

best_practices: Validate dataset schema and label formats before running generation or inference.
Use environment variables for API keys and avoid writing secrets into configuration files.
Review generated hypotheses manually before treating them as scientific conclusions.
anti_patterns: Do not run literature workflows before confirming GROBID and PDF preprocessing are available.
Do not treat generated hypotheses as causal findings without domain review and validation.
Do not clone datasets or install packages in sensitive environments without dependency review.

faq.question: What kind of data does this skill expect?
faq.answer: It expects labeled tabular datasets with train, validation, and test files plus text feature fields.

faq.question: Does it run hypothesis generation by itself?
faq.answer: No. It documents workflows and commands that use the external hypogenic package and related tools.

faq.question: Can it use research papers?
faq.answer: Yes. HypoRefine and Union workflows can use processed paper content when PDF preprocessing is configured.

faq.question: Does it require API keys?
faq.answer: It may require provider API keys when configured to use hosted LLM models.

faq.question: Can it work with local models?
faq.answer: The documentation says the framework supports API-based and local LLM options, depending on setup.

faq.question: What should users review before publishing results?
faq.answer: Users should review data quality, label extraction, hypothesis specificity, evaluation metrics, and domain validity.
